GateDocument.TextWithNodes: "Celebrate International Women's Day with Barbie and Para-athlete Madison de Rozario", "Happy International Women's Day!", "In celebration of International Women's Day on March 8, Barbie is bringing girls around the world inspiring and empowering Role Model dolls'–their very own take on the 'Shero'. Each modelled after a boundary-breaking woman, Australia's 'Shero' is Para-athlete and gold medalist Madison de Rozario. In an effort to inspire the next generation, the 'Shero' doll forms part of Barbie's role model program, one of the many initiatives that make up the Barbie Dream Gap Project, which aims to encourage girls to not limit their self-beliefs. De Rozario is a trailblazing woman who is set to help Barbie do just that with her new doll. "You still see Barbie everywhere and everything they do and how they're changing the industry and how they're changing perceptions and making visibility the forefront of that whole thing, you can see it," de Rozario tells Vogue. "That is so in line with everything that I am trying to push. I think it's so amazing to see a brand using this enormous platform that they've built for all of the right things." Set to compete in the Tokyo 2020 Olympics later this year, de Rozario is just one of the many women who make up Barbie's new line-up of dolls modelled on professional sportswomen in honour of International Women's Day. Among these mould-breaking women are Teresa Bonvalot, a professional surfer from Portugal, Sumeyye Boyaci, a Para swimmer from Turkey, and Malaika Mihambo, a long jumper from Germany. Over a decade ago, when de Rozario made her Paralympics debut for wheelchair racing, she did so as the youngest athlete on the Australian Paralympic team. The sports star has since made three Paralympics appearances, made waves as the first Australian woman to win the London Marathon, taken home a number of awards that recognise her incredible achievements, and has continued to pave the way for women in sports. It's been six decades since Barbie was first founded in 1959. In those 60 years, Barbie has become a household name that has had an astonishing impact on three generations of girls. With its empowering dolls, the brand has highlighted over 200 careers and sold more than a billion dolls - over 60 million in 2019 alone. Now, Barbie is setting its sights on female sportswomen such as de Rozario, and introducing young girls to new sports and possibilities many aren't even aware exist, while simultaneously shining a light on the athletes that are breaking ground for women everywhere."
